coredata.dc:title: Honeycomb hard carbon derived from carbon quantum dots as anode material for K-ion batteries
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yu
Yang, Li
Tian, Ye
Li, Lin
Li, Jiayang
Qiu, Tianyun
Zou, Guoqiang
Hou, Hongshuai
Ji, Xiaobo
coredata.dc:description: Potassium-ion battery (PIB) is a promising candidate in grid energy storage field because of the abundant potassium resource and its low cost. More efforts should be made to research anode material with long cycling performance due to the fading potassium capacity of graphite. Honeycomb hard carbon (HHC) with uniform pore structure and weakly ordered turbostratic domains was successfully prepared from carbon quantum dots (CQDs) through the calcination with the assistance of SiO2 nanospheres. As anode material for PIBs, HHC delivered a high specific capacity of 195.3 mA h g−1 and 67.43% capacity retention after 150 cycles at a current density of 0.1 A g−1. The electrochemical behaviors of HHC electrode were investigated in detail by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) at various voltages and calculation of capacitive contribution, exhibiting favorable potassium storage property.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Hard carbon
Anode material
Potassium-ion batteries
Electrochemical property

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of CQDs; SEM images of (b) SiO2 nanospheres, (c) SiO2@HC, (d–f) HHC; (g,h) TEM images and (i) HRTEM image of HHC.

(a) XRD pattern, (b) Raman spectra, (c) N2 adsorption–desorption isothermal curve and pore size distribution plot (inset), (d) high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s and XPS survey spectra (inset) of HHC.

Electrochemical performances of HHC as anode for PIBs: (a) Initial five CV curves between 0.01 and 3 V at a scan rate of 0.1 mV s−1; (b) first three charge/discharge curves at a current density of 0.1 A g−1; (c) cycling performance at 0.1 A g−1; (d) rate capability at various current densities from 0.1 to 3.2 A g−1; cycling performance at high current densities of (e) 0.2 and (f) 0.5 A g−1.

Analysis on kinetic properties of potassium storage in HHC: (a) CV profiles at diverse scan rates from 0.1 to 1 mV s−1; (b) The linear relationship between log(i) and log(v); (c) capacitive contribution at a scan rate of 1.0 mV s−1; (d) The percentages of capacitive contribution at different scan rates; EIS measurements of HHC/K electrode during 10th (e) cathodic sweep and (f) anodic sweep processes at 0.4 mV s−1.
The designed illustration of honeycomb hard carbon (HHC).

head.ce:title: Honeycomb hard carbon derived from carbon quantum dots as anode material for K-ion batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Power Sources, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha, 410083, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Power Sources
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Potassium-ion battery (PIB) is a promising candidate in grid energy storage field because of the abundant potassium resource and its low cost. More efforts should be made to research anode material with long cycling performance due to the fading potassium capacity of graphite. Honeycomb hard carbon (HHC) with uniform pore structure and weakly ordered turbostratic domains was successfully prepared from carbon quantum dots (CQDs) through the calcination with the assistance of SiO2 nanospheres. As anode material for PIBs, HHC delivered a high specific capacity of 195.3 mA h g−1 and 67.43% capacity retention after 150 cycles at a current density of 0.1 A g−1. The electrochemical behaviors of HHC electrode were investigated in detail by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) at various voltages and calculation of capacitive contribution, exhibiting favorable potassium storage property.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, the rapid development of new energy vehicles and wide application of portable electronic devices arouse huge demand for lithium-ion batteries (LIBs). However, lithium resources are relatively scarce and prices that continue to rise restrict the further development of LIBs, making it essential to seek potential alternatives. Due to rich sodium reserves and similar electrochemistry to that of LIBs, sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) have become a hotspot in energy storage field. Tremendous strides in study of SIBs have been achieved for few years, such as effective synthesis of anode/cathode materials with high capacity and long cycle life. In recent three years, Potassium-ion batteries (PIBs) have attracted widespread attentions. PIBs share an analogous working principle with LIBs and SIBs, called “rocking chair”-type rechargeable batteries [1,2]. Compared to sodium, potassium has comparable abundance (1.5 wt% vs. 2.3 wt%) more negative redox potential (−2.93 V vs. −2.71 V), offering PIBs a higher work voltage [3–5]. Besides, the conductivity of K electrolytes is higher than that of Li or Na electrolytes [6,7]. Furthermore, KPF6, the electrolyte salt of PIBs, is much cheaper than LiPF6 and NaPF6, providing PIBs a lower cost than LIBs and SIBs [3]. Thus, PIBs may be a candidate in large scale energy storage in the future.

Graphite has been successfully applied in commercial LIBs, showing a high specific capacity of 372 mA h g−1 by forming LiC6, the stage-one graphite intercalation compounds (GICs) [8]. Nevertheless, graphite anode exhibits only ∼35 mA h g−1 in SIBs, resulted from the formation of NaC64 after sodiation [9]. The enormous capacity difference in LIBs and SIBs impelled the research on potassium storage of graphite. Jian et al. [10] reported that graphite can storage potassium reversibly with a high capacity of 273 mA h g−1. Meanwhile, fast capacity fading was also observed due to large volume expansion of ∼61% when stage-one GICs KC8 was formed [10]. Besides, more graphitic materials as PIBs anode were studied, such as polynanocrystalline graphite [11], reduced graphene oxide [4], heteroatom doped graphene [12,13], few layered graphene [14]. Nongraphitic carbon, considered as the “first-generation” anode of choice for Na-ion systems [15], also has the potential to be anode of potassium storage. Soft carbon with low density provides better improved cycle life and higher rate capacity than graphite [10,16]. Compared to soft carbon, hard carbon (HC) has more disordered nanostructure, creating more space to buffer the dramatic volume change to achieve long-term cycling performances [17,18]. Moreover, HC usually delivers capacities above 0.1 V, which is more positive than the plating potential of potassium metal, lowering the risk of dendrite formation [3,9]. HCs with various micro-morphologies and chemical compositions, such as HC microspheres [9], sulfur/oxygen co-doped porous HC [19], nitrogen-rich HC [20], have been utilized in PIBs, exhibiting long cyclicity. Although a few exciting advances on HC anode has been made, more efforts should be focused on improving coulombic efficiency and cycle stability.

Porous hard carbon as anode possesses more contact area with electrolyte and short ion diffusion distance, exhibiting better electrochemical properties in SIBs [21]. Here in, the honeycomb hard carbon (HHC) anode material for PIBs was obtained from carbon quantum dots (CQDs) via a hard template method (Scheme 1 ). Numerous uniform spherical pores were distributed in the surface and inner of HHC. Due to its unique characteristic, HHC demonstrated a high reversible specific capacity of 195.3 mA h g−1 at 0.1 A g−1 and 67.43% capacity retention after 150 cycles. Furthermore, calculation of ion diffusion coefficient and capacitive contribution was conducted to analyse kinetic behaviors of K ions in HHC electrode.

The CQDs were synthesized according to our previous work [22]. Typically, sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 8 g) and acetaldehyde (CH₃CHO, 40%, 40 mL) were mixed in a magnetically stirred beaker. After stirring for 1 h, the beaker was put into ambient environment to stand for 72 h. The brownish sticky semi-solid obtained was added to the excess amount of hydrochloric acid (HCl, 1 mol L−1) by ultrasonic dispersion to remove the redundant NaOH. Several times of centrifugation and washing with deionized water were conducted until the pH value was close to 7. The CQDs was obtained after drying at 60 °C for 12 h.

Ethanol (C2H6O, 148 mL), deionized water (H2O, 20 mL) and ammonia water (NH3 H2O, 6.2 mL) were mixed in a magnetically stirred beaker. After stirring for 30 min, tetraethyl orthosilicate (Si(OC2H5)4, TEOS, 12 mL) was added to the beaker and the mixed liquid continued to be under agitation for 6 h. After centrifugation and washing with deionized water to neutral, SiO2 nanospheres were dried at 60 °C for 24 h.

0.1 g SiO2 nanospheres and 0.4 g CQDs were ground together and calcined at 800 °C for 2 h with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 under Ar atmosphere. After cooling to the room temperature, the obtained black solid was ground to powders and added to sodium hydroxide solution (2 mol L−1, 100 mL) with agitation for 72 h. After filtration, washing and drying, the HHC was obtained. The hard carbon (HC) was directly obtained from the carbonization of CQDs at 800 °C for 2 h with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 under Ar atmosphere.

The morphology of this sample was characterized via field-emission scanning electron microscopy (MIRA3, TESCAN) and transmission electron microscope (2100F, JEOL). The crystal structure was probed by X-ray diffractometer (Ultima IV, Rigaku) and Raman spectrometer (Invia, Renishaw). N2 adsorption-desorption analysis was measured on surface area and porosity analyzer (ASAP 2460, Micromeritics). Element analysis of the sample was conducted through X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (EscaLab 250Xi, Thermo Fisher).

Active material, conductive carbon and carboxymethyl cellulose with mass ratio of 70:15:15 were mixed in deionized water and painted on the Cu foil after 24 h stirring. The foil coated with active materials was dried for 10 h at 80 °C at vacuum and then was cut to round electrodes with diameters of 8 mm. The obtained work electrode, potassium metal as the counter electrode and Celgard 2400 membrane as the separator were assembled in the CR2016-type cells under argon atmosphere. The electrolyte was KPF6 (0.8 M) in a mixture of ethylene carbonate and diethyl carbonate with volume fraction of 1:1. Galvanostatic charge/discharge tests in a voltage between 0.01 and 3 V were conducted by a battery tester (CT 2001A, LAND). Cyclic voltammetry tests at 0.01–3 V and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy measurements at a frequency range from 100 k to 0.1 Hz were acquired on an electrochemical workstation (Multi Autolab M204, Metrohm).

Fig. 1 a displays transmission electron microscope (TEM) image of CQDs with diameters of 3.0–5.0 nm. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of SiO2 nanospheres and SiO2@HC are shown in Fig. 1b and c, respectively. After annealing, the CQDs containing rich oxygenic functional groups were decomposed to carbon atoms, which gather around the surface of SiO2 nanospheres by self-assembly [22,23]. HC is the pyrolysis products of CQDs. Thickly dotted SiO2 nanospheres with similar size are embedded into HC. Element mapping images (Fig. S1) demonstrate the uniform distribution of SiO2 nanospheres in HC. As Fig. 1d–f shows, after alkaline washing, SiO2 nanospheres are completely removed, leaving HHC with regular spherical pores. TEM images of HHC under a low magnification are shown in Fig. 1g and h. It can be clearly observed that spherical pores with an average diameter of ∼110 nm are well dispersed inside and over the surface of HHC. In the high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 1i), weakly ordered turbostratic domains can be seen in HHC with an average interlayer spacing (d002) of 0.37 nm, generating more space to buffer the volume expansion and more active sites to adsorb K ions.

To characterize the phase of HHC, X-ray diffraction (XRD) was implemented (Fig. 2 a). Two broad diffraction peaks centred at 24.1° and 43.3° are attributed to the (002) and (101) planes of graphite (PDF#41-1487) respectively [17], characteristic of a disordered HC structure, corresponding to the HRTEM result. According to the Bragg's law, an average interlayer spacing (d002) of HHC was calculated as 0.366 nm, which is slightly larger than d002 of 0.335 nm in graphite, making it beneficial to potassium ion insertion.

In addition, Raman spectroscopy was utilized to analyse the graphitization degree of HHC and the spectra was shown in Fig. 2b. One characteristic peak at 1352 cm−1 is assigned to D-band which is related to the vibration of disordered carbon at the edges of the graphite sheets [17]. The peak centred at 1602 cm−1 is attributed to G-band which expresses the in-plane C─C bond stretching vibration of graphitic carbon [17]. The intensity ratio of two peaks (IG/ID) represents the degree of graphenization [24–26]. The ID/IG value of HHC was calculated to be 0.99, indicating a large quantity of defect sites within HHC. More defect sites can adsorb more K ions during discharge process, providing HHC a larger potassium storage capacity. The nitrogen isothermal adsorption-desorption analysis of HHC was carried out as shown in Fig. 2c. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area of HHC is 75.8 m2 g−1, which is not high among the reported amorphous carbons. The pore size distribution of HHC is depicted in Fig. 2c (inset), exhibiting micropores centred at 0.8, 1.1, 1.4 nm and mesopores around 37 nm.

The investigation on chemical composition of HHC was revealed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The XPS survey spectrum in Fig. 2d (inset) displays two sharp peaks attributed to C1s and O1s. HHC is composed of C with 92.37 at% and O with 7.63 at%. The high resolution C1s core level XPS spectra is shown in Fig. 2d and deconvoluted to three peaks situated at 284.6, 286.1 and 289.8 eV, related to the C─C, C─O and C═O separately [27,28]. The contents of C─C, C─O and C═O are in order of 71.4, 17.5 and 11.1 at%. The introduction of oxygen to carbon material can meliorate the wettability of HHC and enhance active sites for adsorption of K ions [17].

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was conducted to test electrochemical reaction of HHC in PIBs. The initial five cycles of CV profiles of HHC/K cell are shown inFig. 3 a at a scan rate of 0.1 mV s−1 in the voltage range of 0.01–3 V. The first CV curve contains a broad cathodic peak at 0.48 V, a sharp cathodic peak at 0.01 V and a broad anodic peak around 0.40 V. The cathodic peak at 0.48 V vanishes in the subsequent scans, which could be attributed to the formation of solid electrolyte interface (SEI) layer and the decomposition of the electrolyte [20,29]. The cathodic peak at 0.01 V and the anodic peak around 0.40 V are ascribed to intercalation and deintercalation of K ions in HHC. During the following sweeps, the fifth cycle CV curve shows a similar shape to the third and fourth cycle CV curves highly, denoting reversible electrochemical behavior.

The first three galvanostatic charge/discharge curves and cycling performance of K/HHC half-cell at 0.1 A g−1 in a voltage window of 0.01–3 V are presented at Fig. 3b and c, respectively. The first discharge and charge specific capacities of HHC electrode are 416.6 and 195.3 mA h g−1, delivering an initial coulombic efficiency of 46.88%. The irreversible capacity is related to the formation of SEI layer and the decomposition of the electrolyte. The coulombic efficiency rises from 46.88% (1st cycle) to 91.53% (8th cycle). After 150 cycles, the HHC electrode exhibits a depotassiation capacity of 131.7 mA h g−1 with 67.43% capacity retention. The capacity is slowly decreased, which can be relevant to volume adjustment during the potassiation/depotassiation process and partly irreversible insertion of K ions [30]. The rate capability of the HHC electrode (Fig. 3d) was also investigated. The Charge and discharge curves at different current densities are displayed in Fig. S2. It delivers reversible capacities of ∼186, 151, 115, 82, 57 and 42 mA h g−1 at 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.8, 1.6 and 3.2 A g−1, respectively. Moreover, the cycling performance of HHC electrode at large current rate was analysed (Fig. 3e and f). After 100 cycles, it exhibits 74.3 mA h g−1 and 67.6 mA h g−1 at 0.2 A g−1 and 0.5 A g−1, respectively.

Apart from tests on cycling performance and rate capabilities, we also analysed the kinetic properties of the electrodes. The CV curves at different scan rates ranging from 0.1 to 10 mV s−1 are displayed in Figs. 4a and S2. The capacity contribution in the HHC electrode was examined in details according to the power-law formula i = aνb, where i is the peak current, ν is the scan rate, a and b are constants, b-value can be calculated from the slope of linear log(i)-log(ν) carves [31,32]. In particular, b-value of 0.5 suggests an ideal diffusion-controlled process, while b-value of 1 implies a surface-induced capacitive process. Based on different CV sweeps, the log(i)-log(ν) curve is plotted in Fig. 4 b and b-values for cathodic peak and anodic peak are calculated to be 0.70 and 0.68, implying a mixed potassium storage mechanism of diffusion-controlled process and surface-induced capacitive process.

The contributions from capacitive charge storage at a fixed rate can be quantified by separating response current i into diffusion-controlled process (k2ν1/2) and surface-induced capacitive process (k1ν) at a certain voltage on the basis of the equation [24,33]: i (V) = k1ν + k2ν1/2. Rearranging the equation to i (V)/ν1/2 = k1ν1/2 + k2, the values of k1 and k2 can be obtained via plotting i (V)/ν1/2 against ν1/2. A typical profile (Fig. 4c) at a scan rate of 1.0 mV s−1 manifests the separation of the capacitive contribution (red region) from the total capacity. The capacitive capacity of HHC accounts for 46.9% at a low scan rate of 0.2 mV s−1. As the scan rate rising to 1.0 mV s−1, the fraction of capacitive capacity increases to 69.8%. Although being in the majority of the total capacity, capacitive capacity is at a disadvantage when the potential is below 0.7 V. This is because the potential of K ions insertion/extraction into/from HHC, a diffusion-controlled process, is under 0.7 V and it corresponds to the observation in charge/discharge curves of HHC electrode.

Besides, the diffusion coefficient of K ions can be evaluated by utilizing CV analysis according to Randles–Sevcik equation [34]: I p = 2.69 × 105 n3/2 A D1/2 ν1/2 CK+, where I p is the peak current (A), n is the number of the transferred electrons, A is the electrode area (cm2), ν is the scan rate (V s−1), and CK+ is the concentration of K ions in electrolyte (mol cm−3). A satisfactory linear correlation between I p and ν1/2 is shown in Fig. S4. The values of DK+ is calculated to be 5.44 × 10−9 cm2 s−1 for cathodic peak and 8.37 × 10−10 cm2 s−1 for anodic peak, which are higher than DK+ of graphite (∼10−10 cm2 s−1) [35].

The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was conducted to evaluate the electrochemical process and kinetics of the HHC/K electrode with a frequency range from 100 kHz to 0.1 Hz. Fig. 4e and f shows the Nyquist plots of the HHC/K electrode at diverse voltages in the 10th CV sweep. The voltage points selected in cathodic process are 3.0, 1.0, 0.5, 0.1 and 0.02 V, while 0.5, 1.0 and 3.0 V are chosen in anodic sweep. As reported previously, the semicircle at the high frequency region is associated with the charge transfer resistance at the electrode/electrolyte interface. During cathodic process, the curvature radius of semicircle decreases gradually as the voltage is reduced from 3 V to 0.02 V, indicating the charge transfer resistance decreases. When the HHC/K electrode is charged to 3.0 V, semicircle transforms to similar size with that before scan, showing the relatively reversible insertion and extraction of K ions. HHC/K electrode at 0.02 V has the least charge transfer resistance, which may be attributed to K ions inserting into HHC increasing the electrical conductivity.

In summary, HHC with regular spherical pores distributed in its surface and inner was investigated as anode of PIBs. HHC demonstrated a specific capacity of 195.3 mA h g−1 at 0.1 A g−1 and 67.43% capacity retention after 150 cycles. Besides, analysis on ion diffusion coefficient and capacitive contribution proved favorable dynamic performance of HHC and EIS result indicated relative reversibility of HHC/K electrode. The great potassium storage property of HHC made it a prospective anode material of PIBs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Postdoctoral Program for Innovative Talents (BX00192), Young Elite Scientists Sponsorship Program by CAST (2017QNRC001), Hunan Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (2018JJ3633), Innovation Mover Program of Central South University (2018CX005, 2017CX004), National Key Research and Development Program of China (2017YFB0102000, 2018YFB0104200), and National Natural Science Foundation of China (51622406, 21673298).
